name: Colchuck Peak - Northeast Couloir
watch_out: Narrow 1,200-vertical-foot couloir with frequent terrain traps
Loose ice and rock common—consequences of small slides magnified by 60-80 degree slopes and rock cliffs
Avalanche events channel debris down confined gully with minimal escape
Three fatalities confirmed (February 2023) in northeast couloir

name: Dragontail Peak - Triple Couloirs
watch_out: Three north-facing couloirs, 70–80 degree slope angles
Upper couloir and ledge above Hidden Couloir are primary avalanche trigger zones
Terrain trap: 300-meter fall potential confined by couloir walls
Unroped descent particularly vulnerable
Freezing rain and snowfall can rapidly destabilize upper slopes

name: Alpental Falls
watch_out: High-volume waterfall requires sustained below-freezing temps; poor ice formation on warm days
Base runout hazard: Meltwater and ice chunks at base
Avalanche from slopes above falls during or after heavy snowfall
Ski resort avalanche blasting near climbing area

name: Colchuck Peak - Cornice-Tunnel Summit Feature
watch_out: Cornice collapse at summit exit
Tunnel formation can trap snow and create roof hazard
Exposure to long fall if cornice breaks during passage

name: Mount Rainier - Disappointment Cleaver Route
watch_out: Rockfall from Disappointment Cleaver slopes, especially late season (July–August)
Freeze-thaw cycles loosen rocks; afternoon rockfall peak
Rock fall zones: entire upper section of route
Popular route increases exposure to other climbers' rockfall

name: Mount Rainier - Liberty Ridge Route
watch_out: Notorious for both rockfall and avalanche hazards
Ice and snow accumulation on upper pitches
Exposure to frequent icefall from seracs above
Cornices on summit approaches

name: Mount Rainier - Ingraham Glacier & Camp Muir Approach
watch_out: Concealed crevasses under snow bridges
Ingraham Glacier: highest frequency of crevasse falls on Rainier
Emmons Glacier: secondary hazard zone near Camp Muir
Crevasse positions shift throughout season as glacier moves

name: North Cascades - Mt. Goode (NE Buttress Descent)
watch_out: Descent route gets increasingly loose the farther from summit
Rockfall hazard to parties below during descent
Poor rappel anchor positions; inspect before use

name: North Cascades - Neve Glacier
watch_out: Large crevasses throughout glacier
Unstable snow bridges creating fall hazard
Moat development as season progresses
Bergschrund at glacier head

name: North Cascades - Terror Glacier
watch_out: Very crevassed with broken exposed ice unbridged
Hazard: exposed blue ice sections
Difficult or impossible to navigate late season

name: North Cascades - Challenger Glacier
watch_out: Bergschrund easily passable but requires careful foot placement
Open crevasses present but walkable
Moat development mid-to-late season

name: North Cascades - Hannegan Pass Trail Avalanche Paths
watch_out: Trail crosses multiple established avalanche paths
Evidence of historic snow slides along corridor
Wet slab avalanches common during warm rain/spring melt
Most dangerous during/after storm or warm spell

name: Early Winter Spires - North Early Winters Couloir
watch_out: Mixed rock/ice terrain with ribbon of snow/ice between spires
Anchor failure hazard: pre-existing or poorly placed anchors common
Rappel risk: tying all team members to single anchor not recommended
Terrain varies significantly month-to-month and by time of day
Steep gully descent confines potential falls

name: Liberty Bell Mountain - Thin Red Line Route
watch_out: Alpine granite multi-pitch exposure (6–26 pitches)
Rock descent hazards common on loose terrain
Snow and ice present early season (May–June)
Weather exposure on summits
Traditional climbing: variable protection and runouts

name: Washington Cascades - High-Grade Alpine Rock
watch_out: Choss (loose/crumbly rock) prevalent in Cascades granite
Loose descents common; rockfall to parties below
Alpine multi-pitch climbs: weather exposure, icefall
Route-finding during whiteout conditions
Cornice hazards on ridges and summits
